steps.title: Identify the setup and the finding
steps.content: Biologists predicted that feather structure varies with habitat temperature, but it hadn't been tested in mountains. Barve tested 249 Himalayan songbird species across elevations and found high-elevation feathers have more downy sections and are longer.

steps.title: Relate the finding to the prediction
steps.content: Barve's data show feather structure tracks elevation (and thus temperature), which is exactly what the prediction said should happen. The study therefore supports the prediction.

steps.title: Eliminate by choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'contradicting previous predictions': overreach — the results match, not contradict, the prediction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'lending new support to a general prediction': matches both the finding (link between temperature and feather structure) and its relation to the prior prediction. ✓
  • Choice C — 'feathers without flat and smooth sections': misreads the text, which says high-elevation feathers have a greater proportion of downy to flat sections, not that flat sections are absent. ✗
  • Choice D — 'feather length more strongly than feather structure': the passage never compares the relative strength of length versus structure; this is an unsupported overreach. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
